behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is between HTTP handling, raw-body/signature verification, and enqueue gating. A unit test of hash logic alone can miss wiring bugs (e.g., wrong body bytes passed to verifier or enqueue still called).
behaviors.test_description: Run the webhook handler through its HTTP entrypoint with a real raw request body and an invalid `X-Signature-256`. Assert `401` and assert no queue write occurred.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across modules (handler + persistent queue). Unit tests of handler control flow are too weak if queue persistence semantics are mocked.
behaviors.test_description: Use the real queue module (local persistent backend) and instrument timing/order: send valid webhook, verify queue write commit completes before response `200` is observed.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: This depends on interaction between request path, queueing, and async worker lifecycle. A single-function unit test can miss accidental await/join on worker completion.
behaviors.test_description: Configure downstream processor to block (e.g., promise resolves after delay). Send valid webhook and assert response returns quickly while processing is still in-progress, then later completes.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time concurrency SLA. Event-loop contention, scheduler effects, and real HTTP request handling can cause failures hidden by mocked time/dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service process, create concurrent load with slow downstream processing of prior events, then send a new valid webhook over real HTTP and assert end-to-end latency is <3s wall-clock.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is at the boundary of handler idempotency logic and persistent queue state/constraints across requests. Unit tests can miss persistence-key or transaction wiring issues.
behaviors.test_description: Send the same event ID twice through the handler using the real queue backend. Assert first request enqueues once and second request does not create another persisted queue record.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This tests error propagation/mapping between queue capacity signal and HTTP response contract. Mocking only controller logic can hide mismatch in real queue error shape.
behaviors.test_description: Fill the real queue to capacity, submit another valid webhook, and assert `503` plus `Retry-After` is returned and no enqueue occurs.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: The behavior is in the queue module with its persistence/config wiring (local DB/filesystem semantics), not pure algorithmic logic. Unit mocks can miss actual storage enforcement gaps.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize queue with a small configured capacity in a real persistent backend, write up to limit, then attempt one extra write and assert a deterministic capacity-exceeded rejection.
